title: Gooseberry Fool
subtitle: Cooked gooseberry purée folded through whipped double cream
excerpt: Gooseberries cooked with sugar until they collapse to a sharp, pale green purée, cooled completely, then folded through softly whipped double cream. The original British fool: tart fruit against cold cream, needing nothing else.
sourceNotes: Gooseberry fool is the oldest and most traditional of the British fools, with the combination of cooked gooseberries and cream appearing in English cookery manuscripts from the seventeenth century onward. The gooseberry was for a long time one of the most widely grown fruits in British kitchen gardens, and the fool was among the most common ways of using them.

recipe.batchNotes: Make the gooseberry purée up to two days ahead. Fold with cream on the day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The gooseberry purée keeps for two days in the fridge. Whip and fold just before serving or up to 4 hours ahead.

content.text: Unlike strawberry or raspberry fool, gooseberry fool requires cooking. The raw gooseberry is too fibrous and sour to eat, and the cooking step is what gives it its particular character: a pale jade green purée with an almost transparent, slightly jammy texture and a sharpness that cuts straight through the cream.

The purée must be completely cold before it goes into the cream, warm purée will melt the cream and give a thin, runny fool rather than a thick one. Make the purée at least two hours before you want to fold it. Taste it before folding, gooseberries vary widely in tartness depending on the variety and ripeness, and you may need more or less sugar.

items.prepNote: or to taste depending on tartness

content.text: Put {{gooseberries}} of topped and tailed gooseberries into a medium saucepan with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar and 3 tablespoons of water. Cook over medium heat, stirring occasionally, for 10, 15 minutes until the gooseberries have completely collapsed to a rough, pale green purée. Taste for sweetness and add more sugar if needed. Leave to cool completely, then transfer to the fridge.

When the purée is completely cold, whip {{double-cream}} of double cream to soft peaks. Fold the cold gooseberry purée through the cream in a few large movements, leaving the mixture slightly marbled. Spoon into glasses and chill for at least 1 hour before serving.

items.symptom: Fool is too thin and pourable
items.cause: Purée was warm when folded in, or cream was under-whipped
items.fix: Make sure the purée is cold from the fridge, not just room temperature. Whip cream to soft peaks before folding, it should hold its shape on a spoon.

items.symptom: Fool is very bitter or astringent
items.fix: Add more sugar to the purée before cooling. Taste after adding each tablespoon. Fully ripe gooseberries are pale green and give slightly when pressed.

items.symptom: Purée is very stiff and jam-like
items.cause: Cooked for too long without enough liquid
items.fix: Add a splash of water and stir to loosen. The purée should be a spreadable consistency, not a set jam.

items.cause: Over-whipped before or after folding in the purée
items.fix: Stop whipping at soft peaks. Fold quickly and serve within a few hours, the acid in the gooseberry will continue to work on the cream and can cause it to thicken further.

content.text: The gooseberry fool is the ur-fool: the original version from which strawberry and raspberry fools are later developments. References to cooked gooseberries with cream appear in English cookery manuscripts from at least the seventeenth century. The gooseberry was one of the most commonly grown fruits in British kitchen gardens through the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, particularly in the north of England and Scotland, where gooseberry growing was a serious cottage industry. The fool was the domestic use of the surplus. The fruit has declined in popularity as kitchen garden culture faded, but the fool remains a first-rate use of the short June-July season.
